coredata.dc:title: Experimental and modeling study on pyrolysis of ethylene carbonate/dimethyl carbonate mixture
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2022 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Inc. on behalf of The Combustion Institute.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier Inc. on behalf of The Combustion Institute.
coredata.dc:creator: Kanayama, Keisuke
Takahashi, Shintaro
Nakamura, Hisashi
Tezuka, Takuya
Maruta, Kaoru
coredata.dc:description: Ethylene carbonate (EC) and dimethyl carbonate (DMC) are important chemical substances of the lithium-ion battery (LIB) electrolytes. Although the carbonate esters are regarded as a cause of the LIB fires, there are few studies on the pyrolysis/combustion characteristics of EC. This study aims to investigate the pyrolysis characteristics of EC by performing theoretical calculations and species measurements, and to propose the first EC pyrolysis sub-mechanism. The theoretical calculations performed for a gas-phase EC decomposition at the G4 level of theory indicate that a dissociation channel producing acetaldehyde and carbon dioxide, EC = CH3CHO + CO2 (R1), is an energetically and entropically preferred decomposition reaction channel. The rate constant of R1 calculated in the present study is 7.4 times higher than that of the CO2 elimination reaction of DMC, which produces dimethyl ether, DMC = DME + CO2, at 1000 K. Species measurements were performed for the pyrolysis of EC/DMC/N2 mixtures at atmospheric pressure using a time-of-flight mass spectrometer and a gas chromatograph connected to a micro flow reactor with a controlled temperature profile. Oxygenates (EC, DMC, formaldehyde, acetaldehyde and DME), inorganic species (H2, CO and CO2) and hydrocarbons (CH4, C2H2, C2H4 and C2H6) were measured. Experimental results indicate that the consumption of EC occurs at a lower temperature range (T w = 1000–1050 K) than that of DMC (T w = 1050–1100 K), as was expected from the theoretical calculations. The measured acetaldehyde showed a rapid increase at T w = 1000–1050 K, implying that acetaldehyde can be a good marker for the EC decomposition. One-dimensional computations using the present model involving the EC pyrolysis sub-mechanism reproduced the measured results well. Based on the reaction path analysis, R1 accounts for approximately 95% of the contribution to EC consumption at 1080 K, where 30% of EC is consumed.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Microcombustion
Battery fire safety
Cyclic carbonate ester
Pyrolysis mechanism
Chemical kinetic model

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Potential energy surface for EC decomposition at the G4 level of theory (energies at 0 K including ZPE correction). The decomposition channels leading to CO2 production are highlighted with red lines. “*”: double bond, “CY”: cyclic structure.
Measured (open symbol) and computed (closed symbol with line) mole fractions of EC, DMC, H2, and C1–C2 species for 0.112%EC/0.088%DMC/N2 mixtures at atmospheric pressure.

Reaction pathways of EC and DMC at pyrolysis condition. The black arrows are the major reaction pathways where 30% of EC is consumed (T w = 1080 K) and the red arrows are those where 30% of DMC is consumed (T w = 1140 K) for the case of T w,max = 1200 K. Numbers in the parenthesis are percent contributions of each reaction to the consumption of the parent species at (1080 K, 1140 K). Measured species in Section 4.1 are shown in bold. “*”: double bond, “J”: radical site.

Rate constant expressions of unimolecular decomposition reactions of EC at high-pressure limit calculated in the present study (k = ATn exp( −
E a/RT), units: s − 1, cm3, kcal/mol).

head.ce:title: Experimental and modeling study on pyrolysis of ethylene carbonate/dimethyl carbonate mixture

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Tohoku University Graduate School of Engineering School of Engineering: Tohoku Daigaku Daigakuin Kogaku Kenkyuka Kogakubu

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Ethylene carbonate (EC) and dimethyl carbonate (DMC) are important chemical substances of the lithium-ion battery (LIB) electrolytes. Although the carbonate esters are regarded as a cause of the LIB fires, there are few studies on the pyrolysis/combustion characteristics of EC. This study aims to investigate the pyrolysis characteristics of EC by performing theoretical calculations and species measurements, and to propose the first EC pyrolysis sub-mechanism. The theoretical calculations performed for a gas-phase EC decomposition at the G4 level of theory indicate that a dissociation channel producing acetaldehyde and carbon dioxide, EC = CH3CHO + CO2 (R1), is an energetically and entropically preferred decomposition reaction channel. The rate constant of R1 calculated in the present study is 7.4 times higher than that of the CO2 elimination reaction of DMC, which produces dimethyl ether, DMC = DME + CO2, at 1000 K. Species measurements were performed for the pyrolysis of EC/DMC/N2 mixtures at atmospheric pressure using a time-of-flight mass spectrometer and a gas chromatograph connected to a micro flow reactor with a controlled temperature profile. Oxygenates (EC, DMC, formaldehyde, acetaldehyde and DME), inorganic species (H2, CO and CO2) and hydrocarbons (CH4, C2H2, C2H4 and C2H6) were measured. Experimental results indicate that the consumption of EC occurs at a lower temperature range (T w = 1000–1050 K) than that of DMC (T w = 1050–1100 K), as was expected from the theoretical calculations. The measured acetaldehyde showed a rapid increase at T w = 1000–1050 K, implying that acetaldehyde can be a good marker for the EC decomposition. One-dimensional computations using the present model involving the EC pyrolysis sub-mechanism reproduced the measured results well. Based on the reaction path analysis, R1 accounts for approximately 95% of the contribution to EC consumption at 1080 K, where 30% of EC is consumed.

ce:section.ce:para: Ethylene carbonate (EC), a cyclic carbonate ester, is an important chemical component of the electrolytes used in lithium-ion batteries (LIBs). EC has a large dielectric constant, which facilitates the dissociation of lithium salts [1,2]. EC also helps to create an effective solid electrolyte interphase that prevents solvents from decomposing on the graphite anode [3]. Due to the high viscosity of EC (1.9 mPa s at 313 K [1]), linear carbonate esters, such as dimethyl carbonate (DMC), ethyl methyl carbonate (EMC) and diethyl carbonate (DEC), are blended as electrolyte solvents for practical use. Since carbonate esters are flammable and are considered as a potential factor in the fire accidents of LIBs [4], their pyrolysis and combustion characteristics are of importance to be revealed.

Pyrolysis and combustion characteristics as well as chemical kinetic models for the linear carbonate esters (DMC [5–10], DEC [11–14] and EMC [15]) have been studied. However, there are few reported studies on the cyclic carbonate ester, EC, to the best of the authors’ knowledge. A theoretical calculation using the density functional theory at the B3LYP/6–311++G(d,p) level for the decomposition of the radical cation of EC by Xing et al. [16] indicated that the most probable reaction would be the formation of CO2. CO2 formation is a typical reaction for the decomposition of carbonate esters, e.g., dialkyl carbonates produce ether and CO2, whereas the mechanism would be different for linear and cyclic carbonates due to their chemical structure. Investigating combustion chemistry of EC is particularly important to understand in detail the thermal and chemical events accompanying LIB fires. As demonstrated by Harris et al. [17], a chemical kinetic model is useful for predicting the heat release rate and chemical products. From this point of view, a chemical kinetic model of EC needs to be developed.

The aim of this study is to obtain a new insight into the pyrolysis characteristics of EC towards the construction of a comprehensive chemical kinetic model for carbonate esters. First, the EC decomposition is theoretically investigated by performing quantum chemical calculations. Then, the pyrolysis characteristics of an EC/DMC mixture are experimentally investigated by conducting species measurements with a time-of-flight mass spectrometer (TOF-MS) and a gas chromatograph (GC) connected to a micro flow reactor with a controlled temperature profile (MFR) [18]. Motivated by the practical LIB-fire situations, the experiments are conducted for the mixture of EC and the linear-carbonate-ester solvent, DMC, in the present study. Additional technical reasons and advantages to mix DMC with EC are described in Section 3. Finally, the validation of the newly constructed chemical kinetic model consisting of the present EC pyrolysis sub-mechanism and a detailed chemical kinetic model of linear carbonate esters, and a further numerical analysis are performed with one-dimensional computation simulating the MFR system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Theoretical calculations and chemical kinetic modeling

ce:section.ce:para: Quantum chemical calculations were performed to investigate the gas-phase decomposition reactions of EC. Transition states and dissociation channels for the EC decomposition were explored using the anharmonic downward distortion following (ADDF) [19] method implemented in the GRRM20 [20] program combined with Gaussian 16 [21] at the B3LYP/6–31G(2df,p) level of theory. The B3LYP/6–31G(2df,p) method was chosen because the geometry and frequency of 1,3-dioxolane, a five-membered ring involving two oxygen atoms, whose chemical structure is similar to EC, was calculated at this level of theory by Wildenberg et al. [22]. Sela et al. [14] applied the G4 combined method implemented in Gaussian 09 to the DEC decomposition. Based on the results obtained by GRRM20, the stationary points and transition states were recalculated using the G4 method implemented in Gaussian 16 in the present study.

The calculated potential energy surface for the EC decomposition at the G4 level of theory is illustrated in Fig. 1 . The calculated G4 energies at 0 K includes the zero-point energy (ZPE) correction. The dominant decomposition channel of EC is:
(R1)
EC
→ C
H 3
CHO
+
C
O 2
via TS1 (67.4kcal/mol), and the second is:
(R2)
EC
→
C 2
H 4
O
+
C
O 2
via TS2 (74.1 kcal/mol), both leading to CO2 and C2H4O isomers (acetaldehyde and ethylene oxide). The structures of TS1 and TS2 are presented in Supplementary Material 1 (Fig. S1 and Table S1). There is another decomposition channel leading to CO2 and the C2H4O isomer, vinyl alcohol, via TS4 (93.8 kcal/mol):
(R3)
EC
→
C 2
H 3
OH +
C
O 2
however, its barrier is much higher than that of R1 and R2 by 26.4 kcal/mol and 19.7 kcal/mol, respectively. Although a dissociation channel via TS3 shows the third lowest barrier height, it is still higher than that of TS1 by 19.3 kcal/mol and TS2 by 13.0 kcal/mol. R1 and R2 are, therefore, clearly the energetically preferred unimolecular decomposition channels for the EC decomposition. It is also worth mentioning that R1 is the entropically preferred channel for the EC decomposition.

Comparing to the theoretical calculations of the decomposition of linear carbonate esters, a four-center CO2 elimination reaction is the dominant channel for DMC decomposition [8,23]:
(R4)
DMC
→
C
H 3
OC
H 3
+
C
O 2

The barrier height is reported to be 71.1 kcal/mol at CCSD(T)/cc-pV ∞ Z//M06–2X/cc-pvtz level of theory by Peukert et al. [23] and 71.0 kcal/mol at CCSD(T)-F12/VTZ-F12//QCISD/cc-pVTZ level of theory by Zhang et al. [24], which are in between R1 and R2. For the decomposition of DEC and EMC, whose chemical structure involves an ethyl ester group, the dominant decomposition channel is known to be a retro-ene reaction, a six-center reaction producing C2H4 and alkoxy acid [13,14]. The alkoxy acid then rapidly decomposes into CO2 and alcohol through a four-center elimination reaction [25,26]. CO2 elimination reactions for the DEC and EMC decompositions are minor channels as their energy barriers are much higher than those of the six-center elimination reactions of C2H4, e.g., the barrier height of the CO2 elimination reaction is approximately 20 kcal/mol higher than that of the elimination reaction of C2H4 in the case of DEC (at the CCSD(T)-F12/cc-pVTZ-F12//B3LYP/cc-pVTZ level [13] and G4 level [14]).

Decomposition of the radical of EC (1,3-dioxolan-2-one-4-yl; CY(CJOC*OOC)) was also investigated using the GRRM method at the B3LYP/6–31G(2df,p) level of theory. The most dominant decomposition pathway was a dissociation channel producing CO2 and CH2CHO:
(R5)
CY
(
CJOC * OOC
)
→
C
H 2
CHO
+
C
O 2
whose barrier height was 23.6 kcal/mol. This ring-opening reaction undergoes via a primary bond dissociation of CC–O at the β site of the radical site, followed by the CO–C bond dissociation at the other β site. The second energetically preferred decomposition channel of the EC radical was the ring-opening reaction occurring at the CO–C bond:
(R6)
CY
(
CJOC * OOC
)
→
O * CCOCJ * O

The barrier height of R6 was found to be much higher than that of R5 by 12.9 kcal/mol, calculated at the G4 level of theory. Based on the quantum chemical calculations, R5 is found to be an energetically favored ring-opening channel for the decomposition of the EC radical.

The EC pyrolysis was modeled based on a detailed chemical kinetic model for linear carbonate esters (DMC, DEC and EMC) [15]. The linear carbonate esters model [15] was developed based on a detailed chemical kinetic model of DEC by Nakamura et al. [11], which implements AramcoMech 1.3 [27] as the C1–C4 chemistry, and a sub-mechanism of DMC by Alexandrino et al. [9] and a sub-mechanism of EMC by Takahashi et al. [15].

Based on the theoretical calculations for the EC decomposition, the reactions leading to the CO2 formation were found to be the energetically favored channels. These unimolecular decomposition reactions of EC that produce CO2 were considered into the present chemical kinetic model. The high-pressure-limit rate constants of R1–R3 were calculated for the temperature range of 300–3000 K using GPOP [28] software. The calculated rate constants are presented in Table 1 . The calculated rate constant of R1 for EC is 0.96 s − 1 at 1000 K, while that of R4 for DMC from literature [8] is 0.13 s − 1 at 1000 K. In addition, the values of R2 and R3 are 0.021 s − 1 and 1.8 × 10−7 s − 1 at 1000 K, respectively, implying that R2 and R3 have little contribution to the EC decomposition at intermediate temperatures (see Fig. S2 in Supplementary Material 1 for the comparison). Note that although the pressure dependence of R1–R3 were calculated with the RRKM theory using SSUMES [29] software, there was little difference in R1’s rate constant between the high-pressure limit and 1 atm up to 1200 K as shown in Fig. S2 in Supplementary Material 1. Thus, the authors simply applied the high-pressure-limit rate constants in the present work. Based on the analogy to 1,3-dioxolane, rate constant parameters of the H-atom abstraction reactions from EC by H, O, OH, HO2, CH3, CH3O, CH3O2 and C2H5 were taken from the work of Wildenberg et al. [22]. Rate constants of the ring-opening reactions of the radical of EC (R5 and R6) were calculated using the GPOP [28] software. Subsequent reactions to R6 were taken from Wildenberg et al. [22]. The thermodynamic parameters of EC and related species were calculated using THERM [30] software. The THERM code is based on Benson's group additivity method [31], which is widely applied to many programs to estimate the thermodynamic parameters [32]. It is worth mentioning that the consistency of the thermodynamic parameters in a whole chemical kinetic model is highly important. The THERM group values were taken from KUCRS [33] software. The transport parameters of EC and related species were calculated using the KUCRS [33] software. These newly added chemical reactions, thermochemical and transport data are listed in Chemkin format in Supplementary Material 2. The present model includes 369 species and 2085 reactions in total.

A MFR was employed to perform the species measurements and one-dimensional computations for the pyrolysis of an EC/DMC mixture. DMC was used as a solvent for EC because: (a) the vapor pressure of EC is very low and EC is in a solid state at room temperature/atmospheric pressure, i.e., difficult to apply the partial pressure method, and (b) the DMC pyrolysis is initiated at relatively high temperatures compared to the DEC and EMC pyrolysis [15,34]. The feature of the latter, (b), is expected to suppress the chemical interference to the EC pyrolysis by the decomposition products from the solvents. Although the primary target of this experiment is to investigate the EC/DMC pyrolysis characteristics, the feature of the DMC pyrolysis would have an advantage in the validation of the present model, especially the newly constructed EC pyrolysis sub-mechanism. The MFR system has been successfully employed to examine the pyrolysis and oxidation characteristics of the linear carbonate esters, DMC, DEC and EMC, and for the model construction and validation in our previous works [15,34]. Details of the experimental advantages and computational validity of MFR as well as the methods have been described elsewhere [18,34–36]. Hence, brief descriptions and updates of each method are depicted here.

The species measurements of the carbonate esters, inorganic species, C1–C2 hydrocarbons and oxygenates were conducted using TOF-MS and a GC connected to the MFR. A schematic of the experimental setup is presented in Fig. 2 . A quartz tube (2 mm I.D., 4 mm O.D., 8 cm length) with an inner diameter smaller than the ordinary quenching diameter was used as the reactor channel. An electric heater was used as an external heat source to form a stationary temperature profile on the inner wall surface of the reactor. Owing to the nature of the MFR, the small Peclet number, the gas temperature in the reactor is strongly governed by the wall temperature (T w). The maximum wall temperature (T w,max) was varied between 700 and 1200 K. The T w profiles were measured by a K-type thermocouple inserted from the downstream side of the reactor. The measured T w profiles are presented in Supplementary Material 1 (Fig. S3). The uncertainty in T w was estimated to be ± 5 K. EC/DMC liquid (1:1 by liquid volume ratio, lithium battery grade, contamination of water < 6.2 ppm, Kishida Chemical Co., Ltd.) was vaporized and diluted by N2 ( > 99.999% pure) in a heated tank (373 K). The mixture ratio of EC/DMC and N2 was controlled based on the partial pressure method and was set to EC/DMC of 0.2% and N2 of 99.8% (i.e., 0.112%EC/0.088%DMC/99.8%N2) in molar ratio. Baratron (628F, MKS Instruments) was used as the manometer. The mixture was supplied to the reactor at an inlet flow velocity of 2.7 cm/s at 373 K, controlled by a high-temperature mass flow controller (HORIBA SEC-8440F). The experimental uncertainty largely relies on the low fuel concentration due to the very low vapor pressure of EC, which is approximately 390 Pa at 362 K [37]. The uncertainties in the fuel fraction in the tank were estimated to be ± 5% based on the uncertainty of the pressure measurement. The experiments were conducted at atmospheric pressure. For the TOF-MS measurements, exhaust gasses from the downstream exit of the reactor were directly introduced into the TOF-MS. For the GC measurements, the exhaust gasses were sampled online and introduced into the GC. To prevent the condensation of the fuels and water vapor, all the lines from the heated tank to the reactor and the sampling line were kept at 373 K.

The carbonate esters (EC and DMC) were measured using TOF-MS. The ionization voltage was set to 70 eV, and the time resolution was 0.5 s. The signals at m/z 88 and 90, molecular ions, were traced for EC and DMC, respectively. EC and DMC were quantified by the absolute calibration method. The uncertainty in the species measurement of EC and DMC was approximately ± 20% and ± 15%, respectively, due to the relatively low signal intensities. Inorganic species (H2, CO and CO2) and C1–C2 hydrocarbons (CH4, C2H2, C2H4 and C2H6) were measured using the GC equipped with a MICROPACKED-ST column (1.0 mm I.D., 3.0 m, 10 mm Film). A TC-BOND U capillary column (0.25 mm I.D., 30 m, 8.0 mm Film) was used to measure the oxygenates (formaldehyde, acetaldehyde and dimethyl ether (DME)). The sampling volume was set to 500 μl. A dielectric-barrier discharge ionization detector was used, and its temperature was set to 483 K. Helium ( > 99.99995% pure) was used as the carrier gas. Standard gasses were used for the quantification of inorganic species, C1–C2 hydrocarbons and DME. The measured signal intensities of the aldehydes were normalized by each peak value. The uncertainty in the species measurement with the GC was approximately ± 3%. Experiments were repeated more than three times for each condition, and the uncertainties in the measured mole fractions due to the repetition of species measurements with the TOF-MS or GC are indicated as error bars in Fig. 3.

The flow field in the MFR at low inlet flow velocity conditions (approximately a few cm/s) can be assumed to be a reactive flow without a boundary layer. One-dimensional computation is feasible for modeling the MFR system at low inlet flow velocity conditions because of the small multi-dimensional effect on computational heat release and species profiles [36]. The PREMIX code with an additional term in the gas-phase energy equation [18], i.e., a convective heat transfer term between the gas and the reactor inner wall, was applied to perform the one-dimensional steady-state computation. The same conditions in the experiments (mixture composition, inlet flow velocity, T w profiles and pressure) were applied to the computations. A comparison between the measured T w profiles and those used in the computations is shown in Fig. S3 (the T w-profile data for computations can be found in our previous works [15,34]). The computational domain was set to 0–10 cm, and the computational species mole fractions at x = 10 cm were compared with the experimental results. As for the chemical kinetic model, the newly constructed model as described in Section 2.2 (the present EC pyrolysis sub-mechanism + the model for the linear carbonate esters (DMC, DEC and EMC) [15,38]) was used.

The experimental characteristics of the pyrolysis of the EC/DMC mixture and the validation of the present model are discussed based on the species evolution. A numerical analysis is then performed in the following section.

Reactants (i.e., EC and DMC), H2, CO, CO2 and C1–C2 hydrocarbons were measured by TOF-MS and a GC at T w,max = 700–1200 K, while the other oxygenates (formaldehyde, acetaldehyde and DME) were measured by a GC at T w,max = 900–1200 K. The experimental carbon balance at T w,max = 1200 K was approximately 104%, excepting the aldehydes that almost disappeared at T w,max = 1200 K. At the same condition, the calculated carbon balance was approximately 97.1% without the aldehydes. Moreover, the calculated carbon balances with the aldehydes accounted for more than 98.9% at all the explored conditions. The carbon balance implies that most products were covered under the present pyrolysis conditions. Further measurements at temperatures higher than T w,max = 1200 K were not conducted in the present experiment because EC and DMC are completely consumed at T w,max = 1200 K and because of the possible soot formation; however, computations were extensively performed up to T w,max = 1300 K. Figure 3 shows the experimental and computational results for pyrolysis of the EC/DMC/N2 mixture at atmospheric pressure. In the following parts, the experimental results are discussed first, followed by a comparison with the computational results.

The measured EC mole fraction starts to rapidly decrease at T w,max = 1000–1050 K, and it is completely consumed at around T w,max = 1100–1150 K. On the other hand, the measured DMC mole fraction starts to decrease at T w,max = 1050–1100 K, and it is completely consumed at around T w,max = 1200 K. The temperature range of DMC pyrolysis in this study corresponds to the results of a flow-reactor experiment with a GC system by Sun et al. [8]. Although the inlet mixture mole fractions seem to be slightly higher than the target values, the present method using TOF-MS seems feasible to capture the overall trend of the fuel consumption. The measured H2, CH4 and CO mole fractions start increasing at around T w,max = 1100 K. The onset temperature of the measured CO2 mole fraction is T w,max = 1000–1050 K, which is lower than that of the other measured inorganic products and C1–C2 hydrocarbons and similar to the temperature range where the measured EC mole fraction starts to rapidly decrease. C2H6, C2H4 and C2H2 are produced in order at T w,max
≥ 1050 K. The measured C2H6 mole fraction has a peak at around T w,max = 1150 K, while the measured C2H4 and C2H2 mole fractions continue to increase within the explored temperature range. The measured DME mole fraction shows a rapid increase at T w,max = 1050–1100 K, reaching a peak value of approximately 110 ppm at T w,max = 1100–1150 K. The measured CH2O also shows a rapid increase in its normalized intensity at T w,max = 1050–1100 K and reaches a peak at T w,max = 1150 K. These temperature ranges corresponding to the rapid increase in the measured DME and CH2O mole fractions overlap with the onset temperature range corresponding to the decrease in the measured DMC mole fraction. The measured CH3CHO shows a rapid increase in its normalized intensity at T w,max = 1000–1050 K, which corresponds to the initiation temperature range of the EC consumption and CO2 production. CH3CHO, therefore, can be a good marker of EC decomposition. The measured CH3CHO reaches a peak at T w,max = 1100 K, followed by a rapid decrease in its intensity. From the species measurements, EC is found to decompose at lower temperatures (T w = 1000–1050 K) than DMC (T w = 1050–1100 K), as expected from the theoretical calculations.

Comparing the computational results to the experimental results, the present model predicts the overall trend well. The decreasing tendency in the computed EC and DMC mole fractions agrees with the experimental tendency, i.e., the consumption of EC is initiated at lower temperatures than that of DMC. The computed H2, CO and CO2 mole fractions are slightly lower than the measured values at T w,max = 1200 K, which would be largely attributed to the uncertainty in the inlet mixture composition. In contrast, the computational result slightly overpredicts the CO2 mole fraction at around T w,max = 1050–1150 K. One of the reasons for this overprediction of CO2 production would be due to the rate constant of the CO2 elimination reaction of DMC (R4). The rate constant of R4 used in the present model is that proposed by Sun et al. [8]. According to the work by Zhang et al. [24], the theoretically obtained rate constant by Sun et al. [8] is higher than the experimentally and theoretically obtained rate constants by Zhang et al. [24] (see Fig. S2 for comparison). This slight overprediction of the CO2 production for DMC pyrolysis can also be observed in our previous study on DMC pyrolysis [34] as well as the work by Sun et al. [8] at the similar temperature range. The computed C2H6 and C2H4 mole fractions show a peak at around T w,max = 1150 K and 1250 K, respectively, while the computed C2H2 mole fraction increases up to T w,max = 1300 K. The computational result slightly overpredicts the DME mole fraction at T w,max = 1050–1150 K, which is consistent with the CO2 overprediction discussed above. A trial computation by adopting the rate constant of R4 proposed by Zhang et al. [24] to the present model was performed to investigate the influence of R4 on the DMC, CO2 and DME mole fractions (see Fig. S4 in Supplementary Material 1), indicating that DMC, CO2 and DME mole fractions were better predicted at T w,max = 1050–1150 K with the modification. The computed CH2O mole fraction shows a peak at around T w,max = 1150 K, which corresponds to the peak position from the experimental result. The computed CH3CHO mole fraction starts increasing at around T w,max = 1000 K, with a peak value of 0.0008 at T w,max = 1100 K. The peak position of the computed CH3CHO mole fraction agrees with that of the experiment. The comparison between the experimental and computational results indicates that the present model reasonably captures the measured species; therefore, a further numerical analysis is performed using the present model.

Figure 4 shows the reaction pathways of the EC/DMC pyrolysis where 30% of each reactant is consumed. The dominant consumption reaction of EC is R1, which produces CO2 and acetaldehyde (CH3CHO). Although the reaction pathway of EC that produces CO2 and ethylene oxide (C2H4O), R2, seemed to be the second most energetically preferred decomposition reaction based on the theoretical calculation, it has little contribution to the EC pyrolysis under the explored conditions. The dominant consumption reaction of DMC is the CO2 elimination reaction producing DME (CH3OCH3), R4. Theoretical calculations of DMC decomposition [8,23] showed that the CH3–O bond dissociation channel is energetically the second lowest channel. This dissociation channel appears in the present reaction pathway, contributing approximately 18% to the DMC consumption at T w = 1140 K, where 30% of DMC is consumed. As referred to in Section 4.1, the conversion regime of DMC does not change with the presence of EC by comparing to the results by Sun et al. [8]. This can be explained with the results of the reaction path analysis that EC mainly decomposes into the stable species (CO2 and acetaldehyde) and has little promotion effect on the DMC consumption. All the reaction pathways subsequent to the unimolecular decomposition reactions of EC and DMC reach CH3, CO and CO2. CH3 then produces the C2 species through the recombination reaction, and the evolution of the C2 species gradually proceeds, as was observed in the experiment.

The H-atom abstraction reactions show a relatively high contribution to the DMC consumption but show a small contribution to the EC consumption. This would be attributed to the unique unimolecular decomposition reactions of the carbonate esters that directly produce stable intermediates after the fuel decomposition, and to the lower initiation temperature range of the EC decomposition than that of the DMC decomposition. The radical decomposition reaction of EC, which follows to the H-atom abstraction reaction by H radical, shows that the ring-opening reaction initiated by the CC–O bond dissociation (R5) is dominant in the EC case. In contrast, CO–C bond dissociation that is similar to R6 is the primary ring-opening reaction for 1,3-dioxolane analyzed at oxidation, low-temperature and high-pressure conditions [22]. Although the molecular structures of EC and 1,3-dioxolane are similar, the preferred ring-opening reactions are contrary, relying on whether the molecular structure involves C=O bond.

The pyrolysis characteristics of EC was investigated by theoretical calculations and species measurements, and the detailed chemical kinetic model for linear carbonate esters was extended by implementing the newly constructed EC pyrolysis sub-mechanism. The theoretical calculations performed for EC decomposition at the G4 level of theory indicate that EC = CH3CHO + CO2 (R1) is the energetically preferred unimolecular decomposition reaction channel. The calculated rate constant of R1 is 7.4 times higher than the theoretical rate constant of the CO2 elimination reaction of DMC (R4) from literature at 1000 K. The experimental results of the pyrolysis of EC/DMC/N2 mixtures indicate that the consumption of EC occurs at a lower temperature range (T w = 1000–1050 K) than that of DMC (T w = 1050–1100 K), as was expected from the theoretical calculations. The measured acetaldehyde showed a rapid increase at T w = 1000–1050 K, implying that acetaldehyde can be a good marker for the EC decomposition. The newly constructed chemical kinetic model captured the experimental trends well. Based on the reaction path analysis, R1 accounts for approximately 95% of the contribution to the EC consumption at 1080 K, where 30% of EC is consumed. For future directions, further investigations on the combustion characteristics of EC as well as other carbonate esters would be carried out to construct a “comprehensive” chemical kinetic model for carbonate esters. These insights into the pyrolysis and combustion characteristics of the carbonate esters will benefit the evaluation and development of fire-retardant electrolytes and safer LIBs.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by JSPS KAKENHI Grant Number 16H06068, 19KK0372, 20J12398 and 22J13787 and JST SPRING Grant Number JPMJSP2114.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.combustflame.2022.112359.
